status.dataSource: Sources: WHO/UNICEF JMP · NASA GLIMS · World Bank GFDRR

dushanbe.name: Dushanbe
dushanbe.description: Capital and primary urban center. Municipal water infrastructure covers 94% of residents. Central hub for national water policy and management.

sughd.name: Sughd
sughd.description: Home to Khujand city and the Kayrakkum Reservoir. Agriculture-intensive region relying on Syr Darya and Zeravshan rivers. Significant irrigation infrastructure.

gbao.name: GBAO
gbao.description: Covers 45% of Tajikistan's territory with only 3% of population. Houses Fedchenko Glacier — the longest non-polar glacier on Earth. Extreme terrain severely limits infrastructure.

khatlon.name: Khatlon
khatlon.description: Most populous and agriculturally intensive region. Highest flood and drought risk due to Panj/Vakhsh river dynamics. Key food production zone facing climate stress.

drs.name: Districts of Rep. Subordination
drs.description: Districts surrounding Dushanbe. Mixed urban-rural water access. Site of Nurek and Rogun hydropower facilities. Vakhsh River corridor is strategically critical.

analytics.title: Environmental Analytics
analytics.subtitle: Water, ice, climate and energy across Tajikistan

glaciers.title: Glaciers — Tajikistan's Water Towers
glaciers.body: Tajikistan holds over 60% of Central Asia's glacier reserves, covering more than 8,000 km² across the Pamir and Alai ranges. The Fedchenko Glacier at 77km is the longest non-polar glacier on Earth — feeding the Muksu and Vakhsh rivers that power Tajikistan's economy.
glaciers.warning: Climate models project 30–40% glacier mass reduction by 2050 under current emissions trajectories.

waterAccess.title: Clean Water Access — The Equity Challenge
waterAccess.body: While Tajikistan is water-rich in natural resources, equitable access remains critical. Urban Dushanbe achieves 94% access while rural GBAO communities average below 45% — a divide driven by geography, infrastructure gaps, and investment disparities.
waterAccess.warning: Without accelerated infrastructure investment, the rural-urban water gap will widen by 2030.

hydropower.title: Hydropower — Strength and Strategic Tension
hydropower.body: Tajikistan generates ~98% of its electricity from hydropower, making it one of Earth's most hydro-dependent nations. Nurek Dam (300m, 3GW) was the world's tallest. Rogun Dam (335m, 3.6GW target) is under construction and will generate enough to transform the national economy.
hydropower.warning: Regional water conflicts with Uzbekistan and Kazakhstan over Amu Darya flows remain unresolved.

climateChange.title: Climate Change — Accelerating at 2× Global Rate
climateChange.body: Tajikistan is warming at 0.2°C per decade — roughly twice the global average. This accelerates glacial melt, alters seasonal river timing, increases GLOF (Glacial Lake Outburst Flood) frequency, and creates paradoxical water scarcity: more winter floods, drier summers.
climateChange.warning: Khatlon's irrigation deficit is projected to reach 40% by 2040 without climate adaptation measures.

rivers.title: River Systems — Arteries of Life
rivers.body: The Vakhsh and Panj rivers (forming the Amu Darya) are the backbone of Tajikistan's water system. The Zeravshan feeds Sughd's agriculture. The Syr Darya drains the north. Together they carry roughly 64 km³ annually — though this is declining due to glacier retreat and evaporation increase.
rivers.warning: Annual river discharge has decreased ~11% since 1990 — a trend accelerating with climate change.

ai.placeholder: Ask about a region, river or indicator…
ai.welcome: I help interpret the data on this map — water access, river discharge, glacier change, hydropower and climate risk across Tajikistan's five regions. Pick a question below or ask your own.
